narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a part-time study plan and a lower-middle family income background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this law school admission decision. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 47% probability that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by two key factors in your academic record that stood out as pushing the model toward expecting a failure. Your LSAT score was the single biggest influence, as it fell well below the typical levels seen in students who pass the bar, significantly raising the model's concern. Similarly, your third-year school decile ranking was among the top concerns, being lower than average and signaling weaker performance relative to peers who succeed. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided a counterbalance, as it was closer to the norm for successful students and nudged the prediction toward expecting a pass. Your lower-middle family income also helped in this way, offering some protective influence compared to higher-income applicants who sometimes face different pressures. Being in the part-time track, which is less common than full-time (only about 7% of students), worked slightly in your favor too, as did your first-year decile, both suggesting potential for success that tempered the overall risk. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger LSATs and consistent decile rankings around the middle or higher, your profile appeared riskier in those core areas despite these strengths. While this decision is tough, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that key metric, strengthening your later-year grades, or exploring programs with more flexible admissions that value part-time commitments like yours. We encourage you to seek feedback from advisors and prepare a stronger reapplication, as many students in similar situations turn things around with targeted improvements. Your determination as a white male from a modest background can be a real asset in building resilience for the legal field ahead. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
